claims.claim_text: Kubeflow is a collection of Kubernetes-native ML projects including notebooks, pipelines, Katib, training operators, KServe, profiles, namespaces, dashboard, object/artifact storage, and workload components.
claims.span_hint: section: Start With The Problem Kubeflow Solves
claims.rationale: The Kubeflow manifests repository describes the platform as composed of multiple Kubeflow components and common services installed through manifests.

claims.claim_text: Kubeflow Notebooks run interactive development environments for AI, ML, and data workloads on Kubernetes, with support for JupyterLab, RStudio, and VS Code/code-server.
claims.rationale: The Kubeflow Notebooks repository directly states the Kubernetes notebook capability and lists supported interactive environments.

claims.claim_text: Kubeflow Pipelines builds container-based ML workflows on Kubernetes, compiles Python-authored pipelines to IR YAML, passes parameters and artifacts between components, and manages runs, experiments, artifacts, and caching.
claims.span_hint: sections: Kubeflow Pipelines As The Backbone; Did You Know
claims.rationale: The KFP overview directly describes container-based workflows, IR YAML compilation, artifacts, runs, experiments, and caching.

claims.claim_text: Kubeflow Pipelines can be deployed standalone with supplied Kustomize manifests, and the UI can be accessed locally by port-forwarding svc/ml-pipeline-ui to port 8080.
claims.rationale: The KFP installation page gives standalone Kustomize deployment commands and the ml-pipeline-ui port-forward command.

claims.claim_text: The upstream Kubeflow manifests repository assembles official Kubeflow components and common services, supports Kustomize deployment, and documents retrying apply commands while CRDs/controllers become ready.
claims.rationale: The manifests README lists official components/common services, Kustomize deployment, and the retry loop for kubectl apply.

claims.claim_text: Katib supports hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, neural architecture search, and algorithms such as random search, grid search, Bayesian optimization, TPE, CMA-ES, Hyperband, ENAS, and DARTS.
claims.span_hint: sections: Hyperparameter Tuning With Katib; Did You Know
claims.rationale: The Katib repository README directly lists these supported tuning/NAS/early-stopping capabilities and algorithms.

claims.claim_text: Katib experiments depend on an objective metric and metrics collector behavior; training code must emit the expected metric for Katib to optimize it.
claims.rationale: Kubeflow's Katib metrics collector documentation is the direct primary source, but kubeflow.org is not currently on the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Kubeflow training operators / Trainer provide Kubernetes-native distributed training support for frameworks such as PyTorch and TensorFlow, including custom resources such as PyTorchJob and TFJob in the legacy v1 operator.
claims.rationale: The Trainer repository covers distributed training across frameworks, while Kubeflow TFJob/PyTorchJob docs are the direct source for the legacy custom resources.

claims.claim_text: KServe provides Kubernetes-native model serving through InferenceService resources, supports frameworks such as scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch, XGBoost, and ONNX, and includes canary rollout capability.
claims.rationale: The KServe repository directly describes multi-framework serving, InferenceService documentation entry points, and canary rollout support.

claims.claim_text: Kubeflow Profiles provide namespace-level isolation and manage namespace resources including RBAC bindings, service accounts, and ResourceQuota.
claims.span_hint: section: Multi-Tenancy, Profiles, And Resource Control
claims.rationale: The profile-controller README directly describes profile CRDs, namespace-level isolation, RBAC, service accounts, and ResourceQuota support.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes ResourceQuota limits aggregate resource consumption per namespace, including CPU, memory, storage, and object counts.
claims.span_hint: section: Multi-Tenancy, Profiles, And Resource Control
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes ResourceQuota documentation directly defines namespace-level aggregate resource constraints.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes LimitRange can constrain per-container requests and limits and set default request/limit values in a namespace.
claims.span_hint: section: Multi-Tenancy, Profiles, And Resource Control
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes LimitRange documentation directly describes default requests/limits and min/max resource constraints.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes exposes GPUs such as NVIDIA GPUs as schedulable extended resources through device plugins, commonly using resource names such as nvidia.com/gpu.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes GPU scheduling documentation directly explains device plugins and nvidia.com/gpu-style resource exposure.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes RBAC and service account design should follow least privilege; overly broad workload permissions can expose Secrets, ConfigMaps, PersistentVolumes, and service-account privileges in a namespace.
claims.span_hint: section: Multi-Tenancy, Profiles, And Resource Control
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes RBAC good-practices page directly discusses least privilege and privilege exposure from workload creation permissions.

claims.claim_text: Kubeflow began around TensorFlow workloads on Kubernetes and later expanded into a broader platform/foundation of tools for AI and ML workloads on Kubernetes.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes launch blog supports the TensorFlow-on-Kubernetes origin, and the manifests repository supports the later multi-component Kubeflow platform shape.

further_reading.title: Kubeflow Manifests
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for how the full Kubeflow platform is assembled and deployed.

further_reading.title: Kubeflow Pipelines Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains KFP components, IR YAML compilation, artifacts, runs, experiments, and caching.

further_reading.title: Kubeflow Katib
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for Katib tuning, early stopping, NAS, and supported search algorithms.

further_reading.title: KServe
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for KServe model serving capabilities, multi-framework support, autoscaling, and rollout features.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes RBAC Good Practices
further_reading.why_relevant: Grounds the module's least-privilege guidance for multi-tenant ML workloads.

notes: kubeflow.org is not in the current allowlist, so direct Kubeflow docs pages were avoided where GitHub or ReadTheDocs sources could support the claim. The Katib metrics-collector claim should expand the allowlist or be cited from an equivalent allowlisted source if the pipeline requires only supported dispositions.
